era_context: The early-to-mid 14th century: the Yuan Dynasty in China is experiencing increasing instability and peasant unrest; the Hundred Years' War between England and France continues; the Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia; the Avignon Papacy is in its fourth decade; the Mali Empire is declining in West Africa; Japan is in the Nanboku-chō period (Northern and Southern Courts, 1336–1392), though the split has not yet occurred.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (post-conquest sources only)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records outside Islamic and Chinese sources)

events.title: Genkō Rebellion in Japan
events.region: Japan, primarily Kyoto and surrounding regions
events.description: The Genkō Rebellion (1331–1333) was a major uprising against the Hōjō Regency by Emperor Go-Daigo and his supporters, including the samurai Ashikaga Takauji and Nitta Yoshisada. The rebellion culminated in 1333 with the fall of the Hōjō clan and the destruction of their power base at Kamakura. The victory temporarily restored imperial authority (the Kenmu Restoration, 1333–1336) but ultimately led to the rise of the Ashikaga shogunate and the Nanboku-chō period of civil war. The year 1333 marks the decisive military victory that ended Hōjō dominance.
key_figures: Hōjō Sadatoki (Hōjō regent, died in rebellion)

sources.name: Taiheiki (Chronicle of the Great Peace), compiled 14th century
Genkō Heiki (Records of the Genkō Rebellion), contemporary or near-contemporary

events.certainty_note: The Genkō Rebellion and the fall of Kamakura in 1333 are well-documented in Japanese chronicles. The date is secure.
cross_references: Leads to 1336-XXX (Ashikaga Takauji becomes shogun, beginning Ashikaga shogunate)

events.title: Kenmu Restoration begins in Japan
events.description: Following the destruction of Hōjō power, Emperor Go-Daigo established the Kenmu Restoration (1333–1336), attempting to restore direct imperial rule and reform the government. The restoration was short-lived, lasting only three years before Ashikaga Takauji's rebellion (1336) established the Ashikaga shogunate. However, the Kenmu period represented a significant ideological moment in Japanese history—an attempt to revive imperial authority and classical court governance. The failure of the restoration set the stage for the Nanboku-chō period of civil war.
events.certainty_note: The Kenmu Restoration is well-documented in Japanese sources. The 1333 start date is secure.

events.title: Famine and plague in the Islamic world and Central Asia
events.region: Middle East, Central Asia, North Africa
events.description: The early 1330s saw widespread famine and disease outbreaks across the Islamic world, Central Asia, and the Mediterranean region. These were precursors to the Black Death (1347–1353) and part of a broader pattern of climatic stress and disease circulation in the 14th century. Contemporary Islamic sources (Ibn al-Athir, al-Maqrizi) record crop failures, livestock deaths, and plague outbreaks in Egypt, Syria, and the Levant. The exact chronology is difficult to establish from surviving sources, but 1333 appears to be a year of significant mortality in multiple regions.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-hunafa' bi-akhbar al-a'imma al-khulafa' (Egyptian chronicle, compiled 15th century)
Ibn al-Athir, al-Kamil fi al-ta'rikh (compiled 13th century, but includes 14th-century additions)

events.certainty_note: Islamic sources record famines and plagues in the early 1330s, but precise dating is uncertain. The year 1333 is assigned by convention; the events may have occurred over 1332–1334.

events.title: Edward III of England claims French throne
events.description: In 1333, Edward III of England began asserting claims to the French throne, a dispute that would escalate into the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453). While the formal declaration of war came in 1337, the diplomatic and military tensions of 1333 represent an important precursor. Edward's claim was based on his descent from the Capetian dynasty through his mother, Isabella of France. The dispute over succession to the French throne was one of the major geopolitical conflicts of the late medieval period.

sources.name: English royal records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Edward III's claims to France developed gradually in the early 1330s. The year 1333 is approximate; the formal declaration came in 1337. Diplomatic tensions are documented, but the exact timing of claims is debated.
cross_references: Led to 1337-XXX (Formal declaration of Hundred Years' War)

disconfirming_evidence: The Genkō Rebellion is traditionally dated 1331–1333, with 1333 as the year of the final fall of Kamakura. However, some modern Japanese historians have proposed slightly different chronologies based on reexamination of sources. The Kenmu Restoration is sometimes dated to begin in 1334 rather than 1333 in older sources, though 1333 is now standard. The famine and plague events in the Islamic world are poorly dated in surviving sources; attributing them specifically to 1333 is speculative. Edward III's claims to France developed gradually and were not formally declared until 1337; assigning them to 1333 is somewhat arbitrary.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1333 is uneven. Japanese events are well-recorded in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles (Taiheiki, Genkō Heiki). European events (England, France) are documented in later chronicles (Froissart) and royal records. Islamic world events rely on much later compilations (al-Maqrizi, 15th century) and are difficult to date precisely. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia are absent from surviving written records. The year falls in the period immediately before the Black Death's major impact, so plague-related events are beginning to appear in sources but are not yet the dominant narrative.

graph_edges.note: The fall of the Hōjō in the Genkō Rebellion directly enabled the Kenmu Restoration
The Kenmu Restoration's failure led to Ashikaga Takauji's rebellion and the establishment of the Ashikaga shogunate
Early 14th-century famines and plagues were part of the broader disease ecology that produced the Black Death
